name: Nginx HTTP Request Smuggling
description: Nginx acting as a reverse proxy with inconsistent Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding header handling allows attackers to smuggle a hidden HTTP request to the backend server, bypassing access controls, poisoning caches, or hijacking other users' requests.
mitigations: Keep Nginx updated to versions with request smuggling mitigations
Normalize ambiguous Transfer-Encoding headers at the Nginx layer before forwarding
Use HTTP/2 end-to-end between Nginx and backends where possible to eliminate TE ambiguity
Test with smuggling-specific payloads (h2c smuggling, CL.TE, TE.CL) during penetration testing

name: Nginx Trusted Proxy Header Spoofing
description: Nginx configured to pass X-Forwarded-For, X-Real-IP, or X-Forwarded-Proto headers from untrusted upstream clients allows attackers to spoof their IP address or claim HTTPS origin, bypassing IP-based rate limiting, audit logging, and security controls that rely on these headers.
mitigations: Set real_ip_from to only include trusted upstream proxy IP ranges
Use real_ip_header to select the correct header and set_real_ip_from to validate the source
Strip X-Forwarded-For from untrusted client connections at the ingress Nginx layer
Use a dedicated ingress controller that enforces header sanitization

name: Kubernetes RBAC Wildcard Resource Permissions
description: Kubernetes Role or ClusterRole with wildcard verbs or resources (verbs: ['*'], resources: ['*']) grants excessive permissions to a service account, allowing a compromised pod to read secrets, escalate privileges, or control workloads across the cluster.
mitigations: Apply principle of least privilege — enumerate specific verbs and resources needed by each service account
Audit RBAC policies with kubectl auth can-i --list for each service account
Use tools like kube-score or Polaris to detect overly permissive ClusterRoles in CI
Periodically review and prune stale RBAC bindings with unused service accounts

name: Kubernetes ServiceAccount Token Auto-Mounted in All Pods
description: Kubernetes pods with automountServiceAccountToken defaulting to true receive a mounted service account token that grants Kubernetes API access, enabling a container compromise to enumerate cluster resources and escalate via the token's RBAC permissions.
mitigations: Set automountServiceAccountToken: false on pod specs unless the token is explicitly required
Set the default service account's automountServiceAccountToken to false at the namespace level
Use projected service account tokens with short TTLs instead of long-lived mounted secrets
Apply NetworkPolicy to limit pod-to-API-server connectivity to pods that need it

name: Docker Socket Mounted in Container Enabling Host Escape
description: Container with the Docker socket (/var/run/docker.sock) mounted allows any process inside to issue Docker API commands — launching privileged containers, mounting host filesystems, and fully compromising the host from within a container.
mitigations: Never mount /var/run/docker.sock into production containers
Use dedicated CI runners or DinD (Docker-in-Docker) with socket proxy alternatives
Use tools like Falco to alert on Docker socket access attempts
Enforce a securityContext that drops all capabilities for containers that don't require socket access

name: Docker Privileged Container Host Filesystem Access
description: Container running with --privileged flag has full host kernel capabilities and can mount the host filesystem, load kernel modules, and modify host network settings, making any container compromise equivalent to a full host compromise.
mitigations: Remove --privileged from all production container definitions
Use specific capability additions (--cap-add) only for capabilities explicitly required
Enforce admission webhooks (OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno) to reject privileged pods in Kubernetes
Audit Docker Compose files and Kubernetes manifests for privileged: true

name: Terraform State File Exposing Plaintext Secrets
description: Terraform state files stored locally or in unencrypted S3 buckets contain plaintext resource attributes including database passwords, API keys, and TLS private keys, exposing all infrastructure secrets to anyone with read access to the state backend.
mitigations: Use a remote state backend (S3 with SSE-KMS, Terraform Cloud) with encryption at rest
Restrict state file access with IAM policies to CI/CD service accounts and administrators only
Mark sensitive outputs with sensitive=true in Terraform to reduce console exposure
Rotate credentials that appear in state files and rotate state encryption keys annually

name: Terraform State Lock Bypass Enabling Concurrent Corruption
description: Terraform state backend without locking enabled allows two concurrent terraform apply runs to read the same state, make conflicting changes, and write back inconsistent state, resulting in infrastructure drift, resource duplication, or deleted resources.
mitigations: Use a state backend that supports locking (S3 + DynamoDB, Terraform Cloud, GCS)
Never disable state locking via -lock=false except for recovery scenarios
Configure CI/CD pipelines to serialize Terraform runs per workspace
Alert on Terraform lock acquisition failures as they indicate concurrent apply attempts

name: Nginx Server Tokens Disclosing Version Information
description: Nginx with server_tokens on (the default) includes the Nginx version in Server response headers and error pages, allowing attackers to identify unpatched versions and target known CVEs specific to that Nginx release.
mitigations: Set server_tokens off in nginx.conf to suppress version disclosure
Set a custom Server header using more_set_headers module to replace the default
Keep Nginx updated regardless of version disclosure — treat disclosure as a defense-in-depth measure
Remove default Nginx error pages that display version information

name: Kubernetes etcd Secrets Stored Without Encryption at Rest
description: Kubernetes cluster with etcd encryption at rest not configured stores all Kubernetes Secrets (API keys, TLS certificates, service account tokens) in plaintext in the etcd database, making any etcd backup or disk compromise equivalent to a full secrets breach.
mitigations: Enable etcd encryption at rest using EncryptionConfiguration with AES-GCM or KMS provider
Restrict etcd access to the API server only — block direct etcd access from all other hosts
Encrypt etcd backups before storage
Prefer external secrets managers (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) over Kubernetes Secrets for sensitive values

name: Nginx Weak TLS Cipher Suites Allowing Downgrade Attack
description: Nginx TLS configuration allowing legacy cipher suites (RC4, DES, 3DES, export ciphers) or TLS 1.0/1.1 enables protocol downgrade attacks (POODLE, BEAST, SWEET32) that decrypt intercepted traffic or extract session keys.
mitigations: Set ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3 to disable legacy protocol versions
Configure ssl_ciphers to Mozilla's modern profile (TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, etc.)
Enable ssl_prefer_server_ciphers on and HSTS with includeSubDomains
Use Mozilla SSL Configuration Generator for current best-practice Nginx TLS config

name: Helm Chart Deploying Workloads with root User
description: Helm chart deploying containers without a securityContext.runAsNonRoot constraint allows workloads to run as root inside the container, so any code execution vulnerability grants root-equivalent privileges and facilitates container escape.
mitigations: Set securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true and runAsUser: <non-zero UID> in all Helm chart pod templates
Enable readOnlyRootFilesystem: true to limit file system write access
Use a Pod Security Admission policy (restricted profile) to enforce non-root at the namespace level
Scan Helm charts with Trivy or Polaris before deployment
